speaker: interviewer
text: March the 14th, 1995. The survivor is Max Stodel. Renee Firestone is the interviewer in Culver City, USA. And it's in English. In English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Firestone, R-E-N-E-E. F-I-R-E-S-T-O-N-E. I'm interviewing Max Stodel. It is March 14, 1995. We are in Culver City. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good morning, Max. Would you give me your full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Max Stodel, spelled S-T-O-D-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born and when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am born in Amsterdam, the Netherlands, on April the 12th, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Isaac.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- yeah, very difficult to translate. When they slaughtered the cows, the hide came off. And he put some stuff on it to prepare for making leather of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a tanner?

speaker: interviewee
text: That what they call it in English? Fine with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any memories, any interactions with your father as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, because I was with father, I think, the last day before he died because we were on same transport sent to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean when you were a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, as a child, I remember everything. Yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us anything that you remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was, for me, a figurehead. And I had a good rapport because family life in Amsterdam, in the Netherlands, was very good. Very, very good. I was the youngest of seven children. And I was the baby. So I don't think I was spoiled, but I think it was a little bit different than, let's say, the middle sister, middle-- one of the middle children or the oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what sense?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my mother was 49 years old when I was born. And I think it is-- for the age, it's very, very old. And so like I said, my father, my mother owned this-- you can decide. But it was a very, very, very good. Absolutely, there was no divorce in our family, there was no fights. And a real family life, real, real family life.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was wondering whether you remembered anything in your childhood that you and your father did together.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I was a soccer player. And-- and when I was playing soccer, not knowing that my father was-- so cared for me that he even came-- he went out of his way to come to the field to see me play. And he bought me candy to-- because he was a real family man, you know. And I don't know the difference between bad and-- and good because it was only bad, only bad in a sense that I might have been spoiled. But it was very good for me that he did-- that he was like he was. Like my mother, the same way.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Betje.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was spelled S-C-H-A-A-P.

speaker: interviewer
text: Pronounce it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Schaap.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have a profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was always a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you had seven brothers.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two brothers and four sisters. So that made seven with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you name them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure I can name them. If you want me to, I'll go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest sister, her name was Vrouwtje. And I'm calling them by their full names now. Then my next sister was Rachel-- Shelly, we called her. My next one was Eva. Then was Clara. And then I had a brother by the name of Josef. We called him Jopy. And brother-- my youngest brother was Abraham-- we called him Abram.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I went to Talmud Torah, a Jewish school. Only Jewish people. Only Jewish people. I'm going to show you later on, if you are interested, a picture that was taken in 1936 that we were out on a field day. For three days, we went to the train and everything. I'm going to show you a picture with the date and everything on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And rest of the children went what kind of school? The rest of the children, what kind of school did they attend?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother and my youngest brother, my two brothers then, they went both to Talmud Torah. And my oldest-- my sisters, I don't recall that they went especially to Talmud Torah. I think they went to school because before I went to Talmud Torah, when I lived in other neighborhood-- that was up to 1929-- I was born in 1923. I was in a public school. But after school, I had to go to Jewish school for an hour a day, or an hour and a half a day. And I just want to go a little further with Talmud Torah. Talmud Torah was a Jewish school. In winter time, we went home 12:30 on Friday. But we worked every-- we went to school every year Sunday on account of Shabbos was no school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to temple on Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did occasionally because my teacher was a chazzan in the temple, in the synagogue, Yiddish temple. And he kept a list. And he asked the people on Sunday morning, did you go Friday night, Saturday morning, or Saturday afternoon to shul? And he kept track of it. And since he was the chazzan there, so he knew everybody was coming into the temple, so I couldn't lie. And I was going to his shul. I couldn't lie. Yes, I was. If he was there himself, so I couldn't to say yes if the answer was no, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers go to temple also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't think I as much as I did because like I said, I was in his class. And he could take me down if I went than they did. Because I was not the same timings in Talmud Torah when my oldest brother was in because he was older. I think my oldest brother was born in 1914, if I'm not mistaken. So he was nine years older than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents go to temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kind of Jews were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was raised in an orthodox family. And even-- I can see it in front of me that my mother inherited the dishes for Pesach. We had different-- she was kosher. She was kosher. But when my father was out of work sometimes, and the kosher meat and the kosher stuff was a little higher than the regular, she couldn't afford. Not that she did eat chozzer-- far from it. She ate then-- she had meat and butter and whatever from a Jewish store. But not kosher, but not treif, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean it wasn't chozzer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Chozzer? And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, he stayed in bed and home for most of the day but Yom Kippur. He didn't go to shul. He fasted. So did my mother. The light just dimmed. And we didn't-- and we had to-- when it was the day of Yom Kippur. The day of Yom Kippur, then we ate late at night, as you all know that it is customary to eat, I think it was herring at the time, and cheese and special kind of bread, and all that kind-- I don't have to-- that's the way that life was with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you bar mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have an extended family, uncles, aunts?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had uncles and aunts and nephews and cousins, you know. And-- but if you ask me-- if you're going to ask me, I volunteer it to you. I can't remember. I don't. I don't-- I'm almost 90%-- 100% sure that none of my aunts, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe one of two cousins, came back-- who I spoke and now I remember. But from a family, my closest family was 18 people, children and in-laws. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Only one of my family came back. And that is the youngest child of my youngest-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] who is still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the war, were you were close family with the aunts and the uncles.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. I was named after my uncle. And that didn't a week go by or my mother made me go to visit him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you told me that you had an aunt who lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I what?

speaker: interviewer
text: An aunt who lived with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my father's sister was never married. And she was a single maiden. And she went from sister to brother, because she had no family. And then one day, it was my father's turn to take his sister in for a while. And that while was till she passed away. And there's a story to that, the way she passed away also. I mean, she passed away-- passed away-- she was killed in Auschwitz, or whatever. And I might have it on paper where she was-- yeah, I have it on paper where she was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents taken into--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my mother died in 1939 around Purim. And she's buried in the Netherlands. And I-- I know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she die?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a sick person. She was in the hospital. And she always came home. After one week when she was home, she died while I was laying in bed. She fell off-- she was sitting on the side of the bed, and I heard her fall off the bed. My father and me were the only ones in the house. [CRYING] And I heard her fall, and fell dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at the time 15 years of age. I think it was '39. I was born in-- I was about a month before I would turn 16. I was still 15 at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your-- how did your life change after she died?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't want to put my sisters and brothers down. But I was the only one who mourned for a whole year. [CRYING] And I was a soccer player. And I couldn't do without it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went-- that is customary in the Netherlands, if you want to do something that you're not supposed to do while you were mourning, I went to the-- to the-- to the rabbi-- the highest in the-- in the city. And I told him that I was mourning for a year, and I wanted to play soccer.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, since it is a physical thing, he let me play soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it because you went to a religious school and they didn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe so, because I learned in school that you have to mourn and be sitting-- all of my family was sitting Shiva for seven days. And but I was the only one. And I think my father also mourned, really mourned for a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who kept the household after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died in 1939. My sister-- my aunt, my father's sister, was living with us. And my mother had a rheumatic arm, and she couldn't do the chores for the house. So my aunt did it. And she kept doing that, naturally, till [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she went to the camp. But maybe I go a little further than you ask me. But I'm going to come on back later onto how my aunt died, what I said a second ago, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were a child, did you experience any anti-Semitism in Holland?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was--I listen to what happened in Poland, and all those countries where anti-Semitism was very-- really minuscule. It was just a way of life. We lived between the Gentiles. If you didn't live where I lived in Amsterdam, there is no comparison. You can't visualize what it is to live in Amsterdam between Jews and Gentiles because you got bad ones, you got good ones. Jewish, you got bad one and good. And every nationality, I don't care how prejudiced you are, you have to admit that there are always bad ones and good people in every religion, in every race, in every color. And nobody can deny that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you personally experience any?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, really it was just a way of life, you know. If they give you a nickname, that was one ear in and one ear out. You know? And the next day or the same day, you played with the same guy. Because you expecting it, so it was nothing unexpected, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Nazis occupy Holland?

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, what changed then? The family got closer together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, boy. That-- that was something, because my sisters and my brothers, even-- even though they had families, and it became-- like, to my father, because, like I said, my mother died right before war. We were protecting over each other. It was unbelievable-- unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the relationship between Jews and non-Jews change at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because everybody, every Dutch people was hurt by that invasion, what they came in there, because the way they came in. Sure, then there were people who turned their back on the Netherlands and supported the Germans. And they even squealed on people who worked for the good of the Netherlands, for the Jews. And like I said before, there's always bad people. There's no denying that there are always bad people-- profiteers, you know, who wants to make money, and get a kick out of getting even with some hatred, you know, and jealousy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Nazis entered Holland, were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 14 years of age. Let me see. It was in '40. It was-- I was born in -- I was 17 years of age. I was working at the time, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Doing what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a bursar. I was in men's clothes at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything change on your job?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because my boss-- my bosses, two bosses, I had. One, her name was Susan, and the other one was Pozarowski-- a Polish-- a Polish man. His daughter might be still alive in the Netherlands. And maybe she lives in Israel now. But I heard from her once.

speaker: interviewer
text: You worked for Jews? You worked for Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. And the garment industry, where I was working at the time, was 95%, 98% Jewish people. And it was in a Jewish neighborhood, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did that change?

speaker: interviewee
text: That changed when I was sent to a camp in the Netherlands in 1942. On April the 1st, I was supposed to go. But I got married that day because in Holland, you got the pre-marriage 14 days before you married. And I married on April the 1st. And April the 2nd, I was by the first train out, sent to a camp by the name of Kremboong in the Netherlands.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you meet your first wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a dance. My first wife-- there was a dance in the Netherlands. And there were only Jews, because we got more close together when the Germans came. And she was still as a single person. I was a single person. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I took her home that night from the dance. And one thing to another, we got engaged. And then we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know then that the Jews are in danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews were in danger. But it didn't start-- the real danger didn't start till '42. And my second wife's father was one of the first ones who was picked up from the street and sent away to wherever. I think I got it on paper here where he died, and when he died, and how old he was, and when he was born, and all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you personally find out that there is danger?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1934 or '33 when the Germans came to-- the Jewish Germans came to Amsterdam, because there was trouble in Germany. Even a blind man could have seen that danger is coming. I mean, they were up to no good.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did they start coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1933, They came-- I would say 90% of the Jewish Germans who left Germany came to the Netherlands. First of all, it was the closest by country. There's only one border they have to cross. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they settled down in the Netherlands. And as you well know, you might know, that they even became Dutch citizens, because the Netherlands is well known for taking in people who are looking for exile.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the German refugees began to come to Holland, do you remember your parents bringing any to the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was a men's tie maker. And one of her bosses-- one-- a German guy-- a German-- I think it was a German guy who-- he had a factory. And my-- my-- my wife-- my sister worked for him. You know, he made-- she made the ties for him. And I, for one, when my sister got sick, I was 11, 12 years old, when I was sitting off the sewing machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have his story? Did you hear what he was saying about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, but I was too young at the time to get into politics. 16 years, was more thinking about girls than politics.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you were taken to that camp, you just left your job? Or you were picked up from--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a job. And when I was taken to the camp, I had no choice. I was not taken to the camps-- I was sent to the camp, because I did-- I went to the Dutch camp. I had to go on my own to the camp. They told me I had to report April the 1st. I had to leave Amsterdam to go to north of the Netherlands. And I had to take a train. I'll never forget the time. The train I needed was April 2. And the train left 10 minutes after 7:00 in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was this camp called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kremboong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any women in Kremboong?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was only for men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only men.

speaker: interviewer
text: What age?

speaker: interviewee
text: All ages. All ages, because we had to work there. And there was only one man named by the same family name, and even though he was not related to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you working in Kremboong?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I did in Kremboong? There was so much land there, we had to dig in it and to make it fruitful that it can make seeds, some whatever, corn, potatoes. I don't know what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a springboard for labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the fields. How long were you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Kremboong from April the 2nd, 1942 till the middle of July, or end of July something. It was in July, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, and we-- while we was there-- while we were there-- I wouldn't have thought it, even though you didn't ask me for it. When I left in April 2, we didn't wear David stars yet at the time. And then there come a law that you had to wear David stars. So everybody in the camp had to sew on a David star. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] we were-- there was an order we have to go to Westerbork.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the date when the order came out that people have to put on the star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there in April. Left in July. So it must have been end of May or June. I don't know. Either one of the two months because I know I was there for a while. And after that, the star was there for a while, even though when I came back, I got one day-- because my wife was sick in the hospital-- I got one day from the camp that I was-- I was in Westerbork at the time. Yeah, that was in Westerbork. One day, I was allowed to go to see my sick bride in Amsterdam. And when I put on suit in Amsterdam, I left the house to go to the hospital without a David star. And I had to go back, just put a star on before I got arrested, that I wore a suit without a star.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back for a moment to your wife. When did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got married April the 1st, 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And went into Kremboong April the 2nd.

speaker: interviewee
text: April the 2nd, yeah. I was supposed to go in April the 1st. But then when I told them that I got married that day, on account of a pre-arrangement made two weeks prior, I got 24 hours, April the 2nd, first train out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did your wife live then after you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: With whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she stayed with her family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with her while you were in Kremboong?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Kremboong, she visited me there. It was not guarded by Germans, the camp in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it like military service?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Labor force.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no. It was a camp we were able to go in and out, without supervision or anything. It was just a work camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the Dutch-- but the Dutch government runned it on orders of the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went to Westerbork.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do in Westerbork?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] here I go again. Anyway, I'm going to tell you to the best of my ability. Up till today, I am a hard worker, very hard worker. And that was noticeable. I was young and strong, was 18 years old, 19 years old. And I had also to dig in-- minuscule work, what it was. I, for one, was assigned to dig the trenches to build a morgue. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That was no picnic for me to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what you were building?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was told that they had to dig trenches. They're going to build them a morgue.
